title: Watching and Praying for the Coming
subtitle: The Lord teaches us to pray without ceasing, that His coming may not find us unprepared

stillness: Be still, and let your prayer be unbroken.
Augustine says: he who praises God with his tongue cannot do so always;
but he who praises God by his life can do so without ceasing.
gospel: The Lord told them a parable,
that they ought always to pray and never lose heart.
There was a judge in a certain city
who feared not God, nor regarded man.
And there was a widow in that city
For a long while he would not.
But afterward he said within himself:
Though I fear not God, nor regard man,
yet because this widow wearies me,
lest at the last she come and wear me out.
Hear what the unjust judge says.
And will not God avenge His own elect,
who cry to Him day and night?
Yet when the Son of Man comes,
shall He find faith on the earth?
behold: Consider the judge who feared not to offend God,
and was not ashamed to scandalize men openly;
moved neither by the fear of God nor by shame before men.
And the widow, pleading to be avenged of her adversary,
that he might do her justice.
At last, for the sheer importunity of her prayers, he heard her,
but to be rid of his own annoyance.
From this it is plain that some today are worse than he,
who by no fear of God, no shame before men, no insistence
will be moved to do justice.
Yet how much more strongly will God, who is good and just,
hear the prayers and avenge His elect,
who in distress and tribulation cry to Him day and night.
consider: Since we know not what we ought to do, this alone is left us:
to lift up our eyes to You.
The Lord said His coming would be sudden, its hour hidden;
He wills us to be watchful and prepared,
that the Day find us not unready.
Among the things that prepare us is the diligence of prayer,
that we may implore the mercy of the just Judge,
and by humility bend Him and temper His severity,
for the lowly man's prayer has always pleased Him.
The prayer of one who humbles himself pierces the clouds,
and will not depart until the Most High looks upon it.
But the prayer of the proud does not deserve to be heard.
Much avails before God the prayer with tears,
for this God loves more, and answers more swiftly.
As Jerome says: prayer soothes God, but the tear compels Him;
this one anoints, that one pierces.
O humble tear, yours is the power, yours the glory of the kingdom;
you bind the Omnipotent, you conquer the unconquerable.
After prayer He asks of faith,
for if faith fails, prayer perishes:
how shall they call upon Him in whom they have not believed?
In His coming He will find many fallen from the faith,
seduced by Antichrist, and few standing in true confession.
Threefold is the coming of our Savior:
in the flesh, in the mind, and in judgment.
The first two are of love, the third is of fear.
More blessed was Mary in conceiving Christ in her mind than in her flesh.
prayer: grant me to imitate the signs in the sun, moon, and stars,
that is, the examples of good works
in You, the Sun of justice,
in the Virgin Mother, and in all Your saints;
that in the earth of my carnality
the distress of the nations may arise,
the confusion of the sea's sound and the roaring of contrition,
while in me the human desires wither away,
for fear of hell and longing for the glory
that shall come upon the whole world,
when You shall render to each according to his works;
that the powers of the soul be moved from virtue to virtue,
so that I may be found ready at Your coming.

latinJewel.latin: Oratio humilitatis se nubes penetrabit, et donec appropinquet non consolabitur; et non discedet donec aspiciat Altissimus.
latinJewel.english: The prayer of one who humbles himself shall pierce the clouds, and it will not be consoled until it draws near; and it will not depart until the Most High looks upon it.
